topic: A student is accused of plagiarism
raw_output:
[story 1]

The summons arrived via email, stark white text against a dreary Tuesday afternoon. My stomach plummeted. I opened the attachment, and there it was: the accusation. A detailed breakdown of similarities between my submitted essay and a published work. My hands started to tremble, the cheap plastic of my desk digging into my palms. I reread the highlighted passages, a cold sweat prickling my hairline. Every sentence felt like a tiny accusation aimed directly at my soul. When Professor Davies called my name during the departmental meeting, the sound seemed amplified, echoing in the sudden, suffocating silence around me. I just stared at the floor tiles, wishing the earth would swallow me whole.

[story 2]

It happened during the review session. The air in the small seminar room felt thick, heavy with unspoken judgment. When the accusation was finally leveled—a quiet, measured statement from the department head—I felt a strange detachment, as if I were watching someone else’s life unfold. My breath hitched. I could feel the heat rising up my neck, turning my skin clammy. I tried to formulate a defense, to speak coherently, but the words caught in my throat, dissolving into a dry, rasping sound. I focused intensely on a water stain on the windowpane, meticulously tracing its irregular shape, desperate to avoid meeting anyone’s eyes.

[story 3]

The confrontation was a blur of harsh fluorescent light and the relentless ticking of the clock. I stood there, rooted to the spot, my mouth suddenly dry and incapable of forming anything useful. The evidence lay spread out on the table, each citation a sharp shard of glass under my gaze. A wave of intense nausea washed over me, making my vision swim slightly. I could feel the blood pounding in my ears, a frantic drumbeat against the sudden, overwhelming stillness of my core. I managed a weak nod when the committee asked if I had anything to say, my entire body language screaming a desperate plea for oblivion.
